questions.q: Shivaji is celebrated as the founder of which Indian kingdom?
questions.explanation: Shivaji Bhonsle founded the Maratha kingdom in western India in the 17th century.

questions.q: In which century did Shivaji establish the Maratha kingdom?
questions.explanation: Shivaji built the Maratha state during the 17th century CE.

questions.q: What royal title did Shivaji take at his coronation?
questions.explanation: Shivaji was crowned Chhatrapati, meaning paramount sovereign, in 1674.

questions.q: At which hill fort was Shivaji crowned Chhatrapati in 1674?
questions.explanation: Shivaji's grand coronation took place at Raigad Fort in 1674.

questions.q: What was Shivaji's family surname?
questions.explanation: Shivaji belonged to the Bhonsle clan; his full name was Shivaji Bhonsle.

questions.q: Shivaji is especially remembered for pioneering which style of warfare in western India?
questions.explanation: Shivaji built his kingdom largely through mobile guerrilla warfare in the Western Ghats.

questions.q: Against which empire did Shivaji wage a long struggle in the Deccan?
questions.explanation: Shivaji fought the Mughals as well as the Deccan sultanates to carve out his kingdom.

questions.q: In which mountainous region of western India did Shivaji build his network of forts?
questions.explanation: Shivaji's forts were concentrated in the Sahyadri range, the Western Ghats of Maharashtra.

questions.q: Besides land forces, Shivaji is noted for developing what for his kingdom?
questions.explanation: Shivaji built a navy to guard the Konkan coast, earning him the title 'Father of the Indian Navy'.

questions.q: Shivaji's kingdom is often linked to the ideal of 'Hindavi Swarajya', meaning roughly:
questions.explanation: Hindavi Swarajya expressed Shivaji's ideal of self-rule for the people of his land.

questions.q: Which Bijapur general did Shivaji famously kill at Pratapgad in 1659?
questions.explanation: Shivaji killed the Adilshahi general Afzal Khan during their meeting at Pratapgad in 1659.

questions.q: Which Mughal noble and uncle of Aurangzeb did Shivaji raid by night at Pune in 1663, wounding him?
questions.explanation: Shivaji launched a daring night raid on Shaista Khan at Pune in 1663, cutting off the noble's fingers as he fled.

questions.q: By which 1665 treaty did Shivaji agree to surrender a number of forts to the Mughals?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Purandar in 1665, negotiated by Jai Singh, required Shivaji to give up many forts to the Mughals.

questions.q: Shivaji's bold escape in 1666 was from imprisonment in which Mughal city?
questions.explanation: Shivaji famously escaped from house arrest at Agra in 1666, reportedly hidden in baskets of sweets.

questions.q: Which prosperous Mughal trading port did Shivaji sack in 1664 and raid again in 1670?
questions.explanation: Shivaji raided the wealthy Mughal trading port of Surat in 1664 and returned in 1670.

questions.q: Who was Shivaji's revered mother, a major influence on his upbringing?
questions.explanation: Shivaji's mother Jijabai is remembered for shaping his character and ideals.

questions.q: Who was Shivaji's father, a noble who served the Deccan sultanates?
questions.explanation: Shivaji's father was Shahaji Bhonsle, a military commander serving Bijapur and other Deccan states.

questions.q: Which loyal commander is said to have sacrificed his life at the recapture of Sinhagad fort in 1670?
questions.explanation: Tanaji Malusare died capturing Sinhagad in 1670, prompting Shivaji's famous lament about the lion.

questions.q: Around which year was Shivaji born, according to the most widely accepted tradition?
questions.explanation: Shivaji is most commonly held to have been born in 1630 at the hill fort of Shivneri.

questions.q: At which hill fort is Shivaji traditionally said to have been born?
questions.explanation: Shivaji was born at Shivneri Fort, near Junnar in present-day Maharashtra.

questions.q: Which fort, captured around 1646, is often considered the first that the young Shivaji seized?
questions.explanation: Torna Fort, taken around 1646, is often cited as Shivaji's first conquest as a teenager.

questions.q: In which year did Shivaji die, passing his kingdom to his successor?
questions.explanation: Shivaji died in 1680 at Raigad, leaving the Maratha kingdom to his son.

questions.q: What was the name of Shivaji's eight-minister council of state?
questions.explanation: Shivaji governed with the Ashtapradhan, a council of eight ministers headed by the Peshwa.

questions.q: Which Brahmin scholar is traditionally said to have led the rituals of Shivaji's 1674 coronation at Raigad?
questions.explanation: The pandit Gaga Bhatt of Varanasi presided over Shivaji's Vedic coronation ceremony at Raigad.

questions.q: What name did Shivaji give to the new calendar era he inaugurated at his coronation?
questions.explanation: Shivaji began a new era, the Rajyabhishek Shaka, dated from his coronation in 1674.

questions.q: Which coastal sea fort did Shivaji build on a rock off the Konkan coast around 1664?
questions.explanation: Shivaji constructed the island sea fort of Sindhudurg off the Malvan coast to anchor his naval power.

questions.q: Which Mughal general negotiated the Treaty of Purandar with Shivaji on Aurangzeb's behalf?
questions.explanation: Mirza Raja Jai Singh I of Amber led the Mughal campaign that produced the Treaty of Purandar in 1665.

questions.q: After his escape from the siege at Panhala in 1660, Shivaji's safety was secured by the sacrifice of which commander at Pavan Khind?
questions.explanation: Baji Prabhu Deshpande held the pass at Pavan Khind, dying so Shivaji could reach Vishalgad safely.

questions.q: Which administrative land-revenue and Persian-replacing lexicon reforms did Shivaji promote in governance?
questions.explanation: Shivaji commissioned the Rajya Vyavahara Kosha to replace Persian administrative terms with Sanskrit ones.

questions.q: Which spiritual figure is traditionally regarded as a guru and inspiration to Shivaji?
questions.explanation: The saint Samarth Ramdas is traditionally linked to Shivaji as a spiritual guide, though the connection is debated by historians.
